title: Vijñana Bhairava — The Manual for Self Realization
subtitle: 112 dhara.nas from the Bhairava Agama, revealed by Swami Lakshmanjoo with full commentary

summary.one_line: The Vijñana Bhairava is a 112-verse text from the Rudrayamala Tantra, presenting 112 dhara.nas spanning the three upayas. Swami Lakshmanjoo's commentary (recorded 1973-1984) provides the oral trans...
summary.scope: Covers all 112 dhara.nas classified by upaya: breath meditations, void awareness, light and sound contemplation, absorption in the senses, nondual recognition, and sexual yoga. Each dhara.na is mapped to the broader Tantraloka corpus, Layayoga practice, and the daimon path.

sources.label: Swami Lakshmanjoo (revealer), John Hughes (ed.) — Vijñana Bhairava: The Manual for Self Realization (2nd ed., Lakshmanjoo Academy, 2015). EPUB, 8,223 lines.

body.text: Swami Lakshmanjoo classifies each of the 112 dhara.nas by upaya. Approximately 40+ in Anavopaya (breath, mantra, external objects), 50+ in Saktopaya (purified thought, imagination), and 12+ in Sambhavopaya (direct recognition). Many combine upayas. The same goal is reached by different paths depending on the practitioner's capacity and grace (saktipata). This is the most practical demonstration of the three-upaya system in the entire corpus — each dhara.na is a concrete technique keyed to its upaya.

Multiple dhara.nas work through the breath: (1) stopping the breath fills the central vein (sushumna) — the breath rushes down and opens the gate of the central channel; (2) watching the breath as it goes from the heart to the end of the twelve (dvadasanta) and back; (3) the gap between inhalation and exhalation — resting in that still point is Sambhavopaya; (4) breathing with the bija mantras of the 50 phonemes. These correspond directly to Tantraloka Ahnika 5's pranoccara (utterance through the vital breath) and Layayoga's sahita pranayama with the 1:4:2 ratio.

Several dhara.nas work with voids (shunya): (1) contemplate the void above and below until the body dissolves; (2) rest in the gap between two perceptions — the moment after seeing and before naming; (3) the gap between thoughts; (4) the gap between sleeping and waking; (5) the void of the sky contemplated as the void of consciousness. These map to Tantraloka Ahnika 5's ten voids (dasha shunya) and the daimon path's 'fainting into Nothing' (Liber Samekh Section G).

Dhara.nas on light: (1) fix attention on a point of light in the dark; (2) see the light of consciousness pervading all things; (3) concentrate on the light of the sun or a lamp without the glass enclosure. Dhara.nas on sound: (1) hear the unstruck sound (anahata) that is continuous; (2) contemplate the sound of a musical instrument until the sound dissolves into silence; (3) listen to the resonance of the alphabet in the body. These map to Tantraloka's four levels of speech and Layayoga's mantra science where sound is the vehicle of Kundalini.

The Saktopaya dhara.nas work through purified thought: (1) imagine the universe is burned to ashes in a single thought-chain; (2) contemplate that all things are the play of consciousness; (3) see the world as a painting on consciousness; (4) know that the knower, known, and knowing are one. Swamiji emphasizes that Saktopaya is faster than Anavopaya but requires a steady mind that can hold a thought without distraction. These map to Tantraloka Ahnika 13's practice of discrimination (viveka) and the daimon path's 'acceptable illusion' — seeing through the constructed nature of reality.

The Sambhavopaya dhara.nas are the direct path — no technique, only recognition: (1) suddenly turn attention to the awareness between thoughts; (2) recognize that consciousness is already free — no effort needed; (3) the 'lion's leap' — suddenly let go of all supports; (4) rest in the 'I' that is aware of 'I'. Swamiji: 'This is sambhava, absolutely sambhavopaya, pure sambhavopaya. There is no saktopaya in this dhara.na.' These map to Tantraloka Ahnika 13's intensely intense saktipata (tivrativra) and the daimon path's unus-ambo — the recognition that was always the case.

Verses 69-70 (and related) describe sexual yoga: at the moment of orgasm, consciousness is naturally without thought — rest there. Swamiji controversially states these are 'not dhara.nas from my point of view' though Abhinavagupta accepted them. The practice is to use the natural thought-free state at the climax of union as a doorway. This maps to Layayoga's kundalini awakening through the union of pingala and ida in sushumna, and to the Kaula rites of Tantraloka Ahnika 29 (the tantric consort).

Swamiji gives a unique teaching: the five kanchukas (kalä, vidya, raga, kala, niyati) are called 'dhara.na' in the Saiva tantras because they give life to the individual being. They are not limitations but supports — the individual being exists only on the basis of these five elements. Without them, there is only Siva. The grammarian Panini classified ya, ra, la, va as 'antahstha' (residing inside); in the tantras they are 'dhara.na' because they support individual existence. This reframes the kanchukas from limitations to the very structure of conscious experience.

Swamiji explains Abhinavagupta's teaching on jagadananda (universal bliss): the state that is completely unencumbered, where bliss is found shining, strengthened by the supreme I-consciousness of God, and where the six limbs of yoga — bhavana, dharana, dhyana, pratyahara, yoga, and samadhi — are no longer required. This is the state beyond all practice. Maps to Tantraloka's Anupaya (No-Means) and the daimon path's unus-ambo: when the goal is realized, the path falls away.

A representative Anavopaya dhara.na: fix attention on a single object (a pot). Keep consciousness alive — do not let it become dead or let the mind wander to another object. When the mind is fed up with seeing the same object, it wants something new — do not give in. Keep awareness alive. Swamiji distinguishes: if the support is an external object, it is not Anavopaya — it is Saktopaya. Anavopaya uses mantra, breath, place, and dhara.na united together. This precise classification is key to understanding the upaya system at the practical level.

gaps: Full extraction of all 112 dhara.nas with their upaya classification; detailed treatment of the 5 kanchukas as dhara.na
Complete catalog of all 112 techniques with step-by-step instructions

issues.title: Full catalog of 112 dhara.nas needed
issues.description: The current RO samples 10 representative dhara.nas. A complete catalog of all 112 with upaya classification, verse numbers, and practice instructions should be extracted.

issues.title: Comparison with Jaideva Singh edition
issues.description: The scanned bhairava-tantra.pdf is Jaideva Singh's edition of the same text. A comparison of Singh's and Lakshmanjoo's interpretations would be valuable.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Swami Lakshmanjoo's Vijñana Bhairava (2015, EPUB). 10 passages covering the three upayas classification, breath-based dhara.nas, void awareness, light/sound, Saktopaya, Sambhavopaya, sexual yoga, the kanchukas as dhara.na, jagadananda, and Anavopaya practice.

timeline.event: RO created from the EPUB version of Lakshmanjoo's Vijñana Bhairava (pulled from git). This was previously only available as the scanned bhairava-tantra.pdf (28 MB, OCR-only).
